Name: vortex_r2_iiot
Title: Vortex-R2: Relationship-Regularized Tabular-to-Image Transformation for IIoT Fault Diagnosis Under Label Scarcity
Short Hypothesis: Placing anti-correlated (informationally independent) sensors at the image center via a force-directed vortex layout induces well-conditioned Hessian blocks and flat-minima convergence, improving CNN generalization under label scarcity in IIoT fault diagnosis. We hypothesize that Vortex-R2 outperforms conventional tabular-to-image methods (DeepInsight, IGTD, REFINED) and tree ensembles (XGBoost, CatBoost) by at least 3% accuracy at the 1% label regime on at least 3 of 4 IIoT benchmark datasets.
Related Work: DeepInsight (Sharma et al., 2019) uses t-SNE to place similar features near each other. IGTD (Zhu et al., 2021) minimizes distance-rank difference via MDS. REFINED (Bazgir et al., 2020) uses PCA-based distance-preserving layout. All three place correlated features near each other — the opposite of Vortex-R2. XGBoost (Chen & Guestrin, 2016) and CatBoost (Prokhorenkova et al., 2018) are state-of-the-art tree ensembles for tabular data. Prior work on tabular-to-image CNNs for IIoT includes applications to bearing fault diagnosis (CWRU dataset) and semiconductor manufacturing quality control (SECOM dataset). The Fruchterman-Reingold force-directed layout algorithm (1991) is used as the placement engine with anti-correlation weights.
Abstract: Industrial IIoT systems generate high-dimensional tabular sensor data where fault signatures appear as subtle, correlated deviations across many channels. Tabular-to-image conversion enables CNNs to exploit spatial structure, but existing methods (DeepInsight, IGTD, REFINED) place similar sensors near each other — a strategy that concentrates redundant information at the image center and creates ill-conditioned Hessian blocks under low-label regimes. Vortex-R2 (Relationship-Regularized) reverses this: it builds an anti-correlation graph (w_ij = 1 - |r_ij|) and uses force-directed layout to place independent sensors near each other, then applies Archimedean spiral reordering to enforce center-periphery structure with independent sensors at the center. The resulting images have well-conditioned Hessian blocks and flat-minima convergence properties especially valuable when labeled training samples are scarce (1-10% of data). We evaluate Vortex-R2 against five baselines (IGTD, DeepInsight, REFINED, XGBoost, CatBoost) on four public IIoT datasets (SECOM semiconductor manufacturing, Epileptic Seizure EEG, CWRU Bearing Fault, UCI HAR) across four label regimes (1%, 5%, 10%, 100%) using accuracy, Macro-F1, and ECE. Targets MDPI Sensors.
Experiments: REQUIRED DATASETS (do NOT substitute or remove any): SECOM, Epileptic Seizure (EEG), CWRU Bearing Fault, UCI HAR — all four must be evaluated. REQUIRED SEEDS: N_SEEDS=5 (use exactly 5 seeds for statistical reliability and confidence intervals). 1. Evaluate Vortex-R2 vs baselines (DeepInsight, IGTD, REFINED, Raw-CNN, XGBoost, CatBoost) on all four datasets at label ratios 1%/5%/10%/100%, 5 seeds, reporting accuracy±std/Macro-F1±std/ECE±std. 2. Ablation: no spiral reordering (random assignment), inverted weights (correlated features at center), random layout — isolate each algorithmic component. 3. Hyperparameter sweep: T in {50,100,200,500}, tau in {0.3,0.5,0.7}, k in {16,32,64}, backbone in {resnet18, efficientnet_b0}. 4. Grad-CAM visualization on CWRU showing which sensor regions activate for each fault class. Primary metric: mean Vortex-R2 accuracy across 4 datasets at 1% label regime. Success threshold: >=3% over best baseline on >=3/4 datasets.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. Vortex-R2 may not outperform tree ensembles at 100% labels where XGBoost/CatBoost are typically strong. 2. CWRU feature extraction is simplified (8 statistical features x 8 virtual channels = 64 features) rather than using 4 independent physical channels. 3. REFINED baseline uses PCA approximation rather than the full genetic algorithm, which is slow but more accurate. 4. At very low label ratios (1%), variance across seeds is high and results may not be statistically significant for small datasets like SECOM (1567 samples). 5. ImageNet pretraining on natural images may not transfer optimally to IIoT sensor pseudoimages.
